clinicalStem: A 14-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of worsening left-sided headache and nasal congestion. Over the past 24 hours his mother noticed swelling around his left eye, and he is now febrile at 39.2°C. On examination there is periorbital oedema with proptosis of the left eye, restricted and painful extraocular movements, and reduced visual acuity in the left eye. A CT scan of the orbits and sinuses shows opacification of the left ethmoid and frontal sinuses with a hypodense collection between the periorbita and orbital wall. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics and urgent surgical drainage
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and saline nasal irrigation
Topical nasal corticosteroids and outpatient review in 48 hours
Intravenous corticosteroids alone to reduce orbital oedema
Observation in hospital with repeat imaging in 24 hours
explanation: This patient has a subperiosteal orbital abscess (Chandler stage IV), characterised by proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and reduced visual acuity — indicating threatened sight. This requires immediate intravenous antibiotics combined with urgent surgical drainage of both the abscess and the source sinuses. Delay risks blindness from optic nerve compression or septic cavernous sinus thrombosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: IV antibiotics plus urgent surgical drainage is the standard of care for subperiosteal orbital abscess with visual compromise.
Oral antibiotics are inadequate for an established orbital abscess with visual involvement; this is not a pre-septal cellulitis.
Topical steroids and outpatient management are appropriate only for uncomplicated allergic rhinitis or mild sinusitis, not orbital abscess.
Corticosteroids alone without drainage will not clear the abscess and may mask signs of deterioration while the infection progresses.
Observation alone when visual acuity is already reduced risks irreversible optic nerve damage; surgical drainage cannot be deferred.
clinicalPearl: Proptosis + ophthalmoplegia + reduced vision in a child with sinusitis = subperiosteal/orbital abscess requiring immediate IV antibiotics and surgical drainage.
keyClue: Reduced visual acuity with proptosis and restricted eye movements on a background of ethmoid/frontal sinusitis on CT.
commonExamTrap: Choosing IV antibiotics alone — acceptable for early orbital cellulitis (Chandler I–II) but NOT once visual acuity is threatened.
missedConcept: Chandler classification of orbital complications and the threshold for surgical intervention (ophthalmoplegia or visual loss mandates drainage).
redFlagReason: Subperiosteal orbital abscess with threatened vision is a surgical emergency; delay risks permanent blindness and cavernous sinus thrombosis.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman with a 10-year history of left chronic suppurative otitis media presents with a 3-week history of worsening left-sided headache, fever, and new-onset left facial weakness. She reports the ear discharge has recently become foul-smelling. On examination there is a left lower motor neurone facial nerve palsy, left conductive hearing loss, and granulation tissue visible in the posterior superior quadrant of the tympanic membrane on otoscopy. CT of the temporal bones reveals erosion of the tegmen tympani and scutum with soft tissue filling the left epitympanum extending toward the mastoid. Which complication has most likely occurred?
options: Cholesteatoma with facial nerve dehiscence and tegmen erosion
Malignant otitis externa with skull base osteomyelitis
Acute mastoiditis with Bezold abscess
Glomus tympanicum tumour with facial nerve compression
Serous labyrinthitis secondary to viral upper respiratory tract infection
explanation: The long history of foul-smelling discharge, posterior superior granulation tissue, scutum and tegmen erosion on CT, and new facial nerve palsy are classic features of cholesteatoma with bony erosion causing facial nerve dehiscence. Tegmen erosion indicates intracranial extension risk. Cholesteatoma erodes bone via osteolytic enzymes and can expose and compress the facial nerve in its tympanic segment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: cholesteatoma with tegmen and scutum erosion causing facial nerve dehiscence fits all clinical and radiological features.

Malignant otitis externa occurs almost exclusively in elderly diabetics or immunocompromised patients; it causes skull base osteomyelitis but not the epitympanic soft tissue mass with scutum erosion described here.
Acute mastoiditis presents acutely with post-auricular swelling, erythema and tenderness; Bezold abscess is a cervical extension, not a facial nerve palsy with tegmen erosion.
Glomus tympanicum is a vascular tumour presenting as a red/blue pulsatile mass behind the tympanic membrane; it does not cause the foul-smelling discharge pattern or scutum erosion.
Serous labyrinthitis causes sensorineural hearing loss and vertigo, not facial nerve palsy or bony erosion on CT.
clinicalPearl: Facial nerve palsy + foul-smelling discharge + scutum/tegmen erosion on CT = cholesteatoma eroding the facial nerve canal — urgent surgical referral required.
keyClue: Posterior superior granulation, foul discharge for years, scutum erosion on CT, and new LMN facial palsy.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the facial palsy to Bell's palsy — the chronic ear disease context and CT erosion make cholesteatoma the only explanation.
missedConcept: Cholesteatoma erodes bone enzymatically and can sequentially involve ossicles, facial canal, tegmen, lateral semicircular canal, and dural plate.
redFlagReason: Tegmen erosion risks intracranial extension (meningitis, brain abscess); facial nerve palsy indicates nerve compression requiring urgent surgical intervention.

topic: Prognosis of sudden sensorineural hearing loss
clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man presents to the ENT clinic 10 days after waking up with sudden profound hearing loss in his right ear. He describes mild associated tinnitus but no vertigo, otalgia, or preceding illness. Otoscopy is normal. Pure-tone audiometry confirms a right-sided sensorineural hearing loss of 75 dB across all frequencies with a flat configuration. He was started on oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day by his GP 9 days ago. Which factor most strongly predicts a poorer prognosis for hearing recovery in this patient?
options: Profound degree of hearing loss at presentation
Age over 50 years
Absence of vertigo
Flat audiometric configuration
Unilateral involvement
explanation: The degree of initial hearing loss is the strongest predictor of recovery in sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL). Profound loss (>90 dB) or very severe loss (75 dB as here) carries a significantly worse prognosis than mild-to-moderate loss. Recovery rates for mild losses can exceed 80%, while profound losses recover in fewer than 25% of cases. Timing of treatment also matters, but among audiometric factors severity is the dominant predictor.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: severity of the initial hearing loss is the single strongest audiometric predictor of outcome in SSNHL.
Age over 50 does worsen prognosis modestly, but it is a weaker predictor than the severity of the initial loss.
Absence of vertigo is actually a favourable prognostic sign; concurrent vestibular involvement worsens outcomes — so this is not a poor prognostic factor.
Flat configuration carries a worse prognosis than low-frequency ('ascending') loss, but the degree of loss (severe/profound) outweighs configuration as a predictor.
SSNHL is by definition unilateral in the vast majority of cases; unilaterality per se is not a prognostic factor.
clinicalPearl: In SSNHL, severity of the initial hearing loss is the best predictor of recovery — profound loss at presentation has <25% chance of meaningful recovery even with timely steroids.
keyClue: 75 dB flat sensorineural loss — the severity of the deficit is the dominant prognostic variable.
commonExamTrap: Selecting age as the main prognostic factor — it influences outcome but is secondary to the degree of initial hearing loss.
missedConcept: Prognostic hierarchy in SSNHL: severity of loss > audiometric configuration > age > presence of vertigo (vertigo = worse).

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman with a 6-year history of poorly controlled seasonal allergic rhinitis presents with a new 4-month history of episodic wheeze, nocturnal cough, and exertional dyspnoea. She has never been diagnosed with asthma. She is currently using only a PRN oral antihistamine for her rhinitis. Spirometry shows a post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.68 with 15% reversibility. Skin-prick tests are positive to grass pollen and house dust mite. Which statement best describes the relationship between her rhinitis and her new respiratory symptoms?
options: Untreated upper airway inflammation promotes lower airway sensitisation, consistent with the united airway disease model
Her asthma is coincidental and unrelated to her allergic rhinitis
Nasal polyps have obstructed nasopharyngeal airflow causing mouth breathing and airway drying
Antihistamine overuse has caused rebound lower airway hyperreactivity
Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease explains the combination of rhinitis and asthma
explanation: The unified (or united) airway disease model recognises that the upper and lower airways form a single functional unit; chronic untreated allergic rhinitis drives systemic and local eosinophilic inflammation that promotes bronchial hyperreactivity and asthma development. Up to 40% of allergic rhinitis patients develop asthma, and controlling rhinitis with intranasal corticosteroids and immunotherapy reduces asthma exacerbation rates. This patient's timeline (rhinitis preceding asthma) and shared sensitisation (grass pollen, HDM) are classic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: the unified airway model explains the epidemiological and mechanistic link between allergic rhinitis and asthma — they share a common allergic inflammatory process.

The temporal sequence (rhinitis for 6 years, then asthma onset) and shared allergen sensitisation make a coincidental relationship very unlikely; the united airway model directly explains this co-occurrence.
Nasal polyps have not been mentioned; simple mouth breathing does not cause reversible bronchospasm with eosinophilia-type physiology.
Oral antihistamines do not cause lower airway rebound hyperreactivity; they are considered safe even in asthma.

Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (Samter's triad) requires aspirin/NSAID sensitivity as a trigger and is associated with non-allergic rhinitis and polyps, not IgE-mediated pollen/HDM sensitisation.
clinicalPearl: Up to 40% of allergic rhinitis patients develop asthma — the united airway model: treat the nose to protect the lungs.
keyClue: 6 years of untreated allergic rhinitis preceding new-onset reversible airflow obstruction with shared allergen sensitisation.
commonExamTrap: Treating rhinitis and asthma as unrelated diagnoses rather than recognising united airway disease.
missedConcept: Inadequate rhinitis control is an independent risk factor for asthma development and poor asthma outcomes.

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old male presents with a 2-week history of frontal headache and nasal congestion following an upper respiratory tract infection. Over the past 4 days he has developed a painless fluctuant swelling over the forehead, progressive drowsiness, and a temperature of 38.8°C. On examination there is a 4 cm doughy swelling over the right forehead. CT brain and sinuses with contrast shows frontal sinusitis, anterior table erosion of the frontal bone, and a subperiosteal collection with an adjacent intracranial epidural abscess. Which eponymous condition is described, and what is the most urgent concern?
options: Pott's puffy tumour with intracranial extension causing epidural abscess
Frontal lobe glioma with overlying skin infiltration
Bezold abscess with posterior fossa extension
Paget's disease of the skull with secondary sinusitis
Subperiosteal haematoma following trivial head injury
explanation: Pott's puffy tumour is a subperiosteal abscess of the frontal bone resulting from osteomyelitis due to frontal sinusitis, causing anterior table erosion and a forehead fluctuant swelling. The most dangerous feature here is the coexisting epidural abscess causing drowsiness — an intracranial emergency requiring neurosurgical drainage alongside functional endoscopic sinus surgery and intravenous antibiotics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Pott's puffy tumour with epidural abscess is the classic complication of frontal sinusitis with anterior table erosion, and drowsiness signals dangerous intracranial pressure.
Frontal lobe glioma is not associated with sinusitis, bone erosion at the anterior table, or the acute infective presentation described here.
Bezold abscess is a complication of mastoiditis — a subperiosteal collection in the posterior triangle of the neck — not related to frontal sinus disease.
Paget's disease causes slow bony expansion, not acute infection with fever, sinus opacification, and fluctuant abscess formation.
Post-traumatic subperiosteal haematoma would have a trauma history, no sinus disease, no fever, and would not cause bone erosion or intracranial extension.
clinicalPearl: Pott's puffy tumour = frontal sinus osteomyelitis + forehead swelling + often epidural abscess — always image the intracranial compartment urgently.
keyClue: Frontal swelling + frontal sinusitis + anterior table erosion + epidural abscess + drowsiness in an adolescent.
commonExamTrap: Dismissing the forehead swelling as cellulitis and missing the epidural abscess requiring neurosurgery.
missedConcept: Pott's puffy tumour is a neurosurgical emergency because epidural abscess and even subdural empyema can coexist in up to 60% of cases.
redFlagReason: Epidural abscess causing drowsiness is a neurosurgical emergency with risk of transtentorial herniation if not drained urgently.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her parents with a 5-day history of right ear pain and fever that initially improved on oral amoxicillin but has worsened over the past 48 hours. She is now febrile at 39.6°C, irritable, and the right pinna is displaced anteriorly and inferiorly. There is post-auricular erythema, tenderness, and obliteration of the post-auricular sulcus. Otoscopy reveals a bulging, erythematous right tympanic membrane. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis and what complication has occurred?
options: Acute mastoiditis with subperiosteal abscess complicating acute otitis media
Severe external otitis with pre-auricular lymphadenopathy
Furunculosis of the external auditory canal with periauricular spread
Ramsay Hunt syndrome with post-auricular involvement
Branchial cyst infection causing post-auricular swelling
explanation: Anterior–inferior displacement of the pinna with obliteration of the post-auricular sulcus and post-auricular fluctuance following AOM that initially responded then worsened is the classic presentation of acute mastoiditis complicated by a subperiosteal abscess. The mastoid air cells are contiguous with the middle ear; when infection spreads and breaks through the mastoid cortex it forms a subperiosteal collection. This requires IV antibiotics and usually surgical drainage (cortical mastoidectomy with myringotomy).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: pinna displacement + obliterated sulcus + post-auricular fluctuance in a child with AOM = acute mastoiditis with subperiosteal abscess.
External otitis causes canal and tragus tenderness, not pinna displacement or obliteration of the post-auricular sulcus; and it would not follow AOM and antibiotic treatment.
Furunculosis is localised to the cartilaginous canal; it does not produce pinna displacement or post-auricular sulcus obliteration.
Ramsay Hunt syndrome causes vesicular eruption in the ear canal/concha with ipsilateral LMN facial palsy and sensorineural hearing loss — not post-auricular fluctuance in a 3-year-old.
A branchial cyst is an anatomically distinct lateral neck lesion; it does not present as post-auricular swelling with pinna displacement.
clinicalPearl: Pinna displaced anteriorly + obliterated post-auricular sulcus + AOM = subperiosteal abscess from mastoiditis — do not wait, start IV antibiotics and arrange ENT review.
keyClue: Anterior-inferior pinna displacement with loss of the post-auricular sulcus after AOM treatment failure.
commonExamTrap: Continuing oral antibiotics alone for what is labelled 'persistent AOM' when physical signs clearly indicate mastoid cortex breakthrough.
missedConcept: Subperiosteal abscess from mastoiditis is not an extension of external otitis — it arises from mastoid cortex erosion and requires IV antibiotics ± surgical drainage.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old man with severe grass-pollen allergic rhinitis uncontrolled despite maximal intranasal corticosteroid and antihistamine therapy has been commenced on subcutaneous allergen immunotherapy (SCIT) for 3 months. He attends his clinic injection and 20 minutes post-injection develops generalised urticaria, throat tightness, and a wheeze. His blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Intramuscular adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg (1:1000) in the lateral thigh
Intravenous chlorphenamine and IV hydrocortisone only
Nebulised salbutamol and oxygen via facemask
Oral cetirizine and observation for 30 further minutes
Subcutaneous adrenaline 0.1 mg and antihistamine
explanation: This patient has anaphylaxis following SCIT — systemic allergic reaction with hypotension, urticaria, throat tightness, and wheeze. First-line and life-saving treatment is intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) in the anterolateral thigh, as per international anaphylaxis guidelines. IM injection achieves faster and more reliable absorption than subcutaneous. Observation protocols for SCIT exist specifically to catch this complication; all SCIT clinics must have adrenaline immediately available.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) in the lateral thigh is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis in adults — it is the only intervention that reverses the life-threatening cardiovascular and airway components simultaneously.
IV chlorphenamine and hydrocortisone are adjuncts; they have delayed onset and are not first-line — administering them before adrenaline delays definitive treatment and risks death.
Nebulised salbutamol addresses only bronchospasm and does not treat systemic vasodilation, hypotension, or laryngeal oedema — it is a second-line adjunct after adrenaline.
Oral antihistamine is grossly inadequate for anaphylaxis — it treats urticaria but has no meaningful effect on airway oedema or haemodynamic collapse.
Subcutaneous adrenaline has slower, less reliable absorption than IM; current guidelines explicitly recommend IM over subcutaneous in anaphylaxis.
clinicalPearl: All SCIT patients must be observed for 30 minutes post-injection; any systemic reaction = IM adrenaline first, then adjuncts.
keyClue: Urticaria + throat tightness + wheeze + hypotension 20 minutes after allergen immunotherapy injection = anaphylaxis.
commonExamTrap: Reaching for IV antihistamines and steroids first — these are adjuncts; adrenaline is always the priority in anaphylaxis.
missedConcept: IM is superior to SC adrenaline in anaphylaxis due to faster and more predictable absorption from the vastus lateralis.
redFlagReason: Anaphylaxis with hypotension (systolic 90) and airway involvement is an immediately life-threatening emergency requiring adrenaline without delay.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old male factory worker presents with a 2-year history of bilateral tinnitus and difficulty hearing speech in noisy environments. He has worked in a steel pressing factory for 20 years without ear protection. Pure-tone audiometry reveals a bilateral symmetrical sensorineural hearing loss with a characteristic notch at 4 kHz, recovering at 6–8 kHz. He asks whether stopping exposure will reverse his hearing loss. Which statement most accurately describes the prognosis of his established noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)?
options: Established NIHL is permanent; removing noise exposure prevents further progression but does not restore existing loss
Hearing will recover spontaneously over 6–12 months after noise cessation
Hearing aids are contraindicated in NIHL as they may worsen tinnitus
A 2-week course of oral corticosteroids will partially reverse the cochlear damage
Cochlear implantation is the treatment of choice for bilateral 4 kHz notch loss
explanation: Established noise-induced hearing loss represents permanent damage to outer hair cells of the cochlea, particularly at the 4 kHz region (the first to be affected by high-intensity noise). Once outer hair cells are destroyed they do not regenerate in humans. Removing the noise exposure prevents further injury but cannot reverse existing loss. Management includes hearing protection, hearing aids, and tinnitus rehabilitation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: established NIHL is irreversible — outer hair cell loss is permanent. Only cessation of exposure prevents further damage.
Spontaneous recovery does not occur with established NIHL; temporary threshold shift (TTS) after acute noise exposure may recover, but permanent threshold shift (PTS) does not.
Hearing aids are in fact the mainstay of rehabilitation for NIHL-associated hearing impairment; they do not worsen tinnitus and often reduce tinnitus perception by amplifying background sound.
Oral corticosteroids have no evidence of benefit in established chronic NIHL; they may have a role in acute acoustic trauma presenting within 72 hours.

Cochlear implantation is reserved for severe-to-profound bilateral loss where hearing aids provide insufficient benefit — a 4 kHz notch with otherwise normal or near-normal thresholds does not meet cochlear implant criteria.
clinicalPearl: NIHL 4 kHz notch = permanent outer hair cell loss — stop exposure to prevent progression, but lost hearing will not return.
keyClue: Bilateral 4 kHz notch after 20 years of noise exposure = established permanent noise-induced hearing loss.
commonExamTrap: Confusing temporary threshold shift (recoverable, acute) with permanent threshold shift (irreversible, chronic).
missedConcept: Outer hair cells destroyed by chronic noise exposure do not regenerate — prognosis for recovery is nil; prevention is the only effective strategy.

topic: Follow-up after FESS for CRS with nasal polyps
clinicalStem: A 47-year-old woman with chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (CRSwNP) underwent bilateral functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) 8 weeks ago. She has a background of asthma and aspirin sensitivity (Samter's triad). At her follow-up clinic she reports significant improvement in nasal blockage but now has recurrent right-sided clear rhinorrhoea, sneezing, and anosmia returning. Nasal endoscopy reveals new small polyps at the right middle meatus. Which follow-up regimen is most appropriate to reduce the risk of polyp recurrence?
options: High-dose intranasal corticosteroid drops (mometasone) in the head-down position plus regular saline irrigation post-FESS
Systemic oral prednisolone as sole long-term maintenance therapy
Immediate revision FESS without any medical therapy
Daily antihistamines as sole post-operative prophylaxis
Aspirin desensitisation alone without any topical corticosteroid
explanation: In Samter's triad (CRSwNP + asthma + aspirin sensitivity), polyps are highly recurrent post-FESS without aggressive medical maintenance. Intranasal corticosteroid drops (mometasone or budesonide) applied in the head-down and forward position maximise deposition in the opened sinuses and are first-line post-FESS medical maintenance. Saline irrigation keeps the mucosa clear and removes inflammatory debris. This combination reduces recurrence rates significantly compared to surgery alone.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: high-dose topical intranasal corticosteroids in the head-down position with saline irrigation is the evidence-based first-line post-FESS maintenance for CRSwNP, especially in Samter's triad.
Long-term oral corticosteroids carry systemic risks (adrenal suppression, osteoporosis, diabetes) and are not appropriate as sole maintenance; topical therapy is preferred unless refractory.
Revision FESS at 8 weeks for small recurrent polyps without maximising medical therapy is premature and exposes the patient to surgical risks; medical optimisation should be first.
Antihistamines have no evidence of benefit in CRSwNP recurrence prevention; they target allergic rhinitis, which is a different mechanism from eosinophilic polyp disease.
Aspirin desensitisation (in specialist centres) is an adjunct in Samter's triad but cannot substitute for topical corticosteroids, which directly suppress eosinophilic mucosal inflammation.
clinicalPearl: Post-FESS for CRSwNP: intranasal corticosteroid drops in the head-down position + saline irrigation = cornerstone of recurrence prevention, especially in Samter's triad.
keyClue: Samter's triad (CRSwNP + asthma + aspirin sensitivity) post-FESS with early small polyp recurrence — needs maximal topical medical therapy before revision surgery.
commonExamTrap: Recommending immediate revision surgery rather than optimising post-operative medical maintenance first.
missedConcept: Post-FESS topical drug delivery is enhanced because opened sinus cavities allow corticosteroid drops to reach disease; head-down position maximises distribution.

clinicalStem: A 33-year-old woman presents with a 5-year progressive bilateral conductive hearing loss. She reports a family history of deafness and has noticed worsening symptoms during her two pregnancies. Audiometry reveals bilateral conductive hearing loss with a characteristic Carhart notch at 2 kHz. A tympanogram shows a flat (As) pattern. She has undergone right stapedectomy 3 months ago. At her post-operative audiogram her right air–bone gap has closed to less than 10 dB. She now asks about long-term outcomes and what to watch for. Which of the following is the most important long-term risk she should be counselled about?
options: Delayed sensorineural hearing loss due to perilymphatic fistula or reparative granuloma
Facial nerve palsy that develops 2 years post-operatively
Bilateral simultaneous progression to total deafness within 5 years
Tympanic membrane perforation as the most common late complication
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo as an inevitable long-term sequela
explanation: Following successful stapedectomy with a closed air-bone gap, the most important long-term complication to monitor is delayed sensorineural hearing loss, which can occur months to years post-operatively due to perilymphatic fistula, reparative granuloma around the prosthesis, or prosthesis displacement. This occurs in approximately 1–2% of cases and can result in profound loss if unrecognised. Patients should be counselled to report any sudden deterioration, vertigo, or new tinnitus.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: delayed sensorineural hearing loss from perilymphatic fistula or reparative granuloma is the most important long-term risk after stapedectomy and can cause irreversible profound deafness if not promptly managed.
Facial nerve palsy from stapedectomy is a recognised intraoperative risk (due to aberrant facial nerve anatomy), not a de novo delayed complication developing 2 years post-operatively.
Bilateral simultaneous progression to total deafness within 5 years is not a predictable natural history of otosclerosis, and the operated ear has already been successfully treated.
Tympanic membrane perforation is a rare early complication of stapedectomy but not a major long-term concern once the ear has healed post-operatively.
BPPV is occasionally seen in the early post-operative period due to otolith displacement during surgery; it is not an inevitable long-term sequela and usually resolves with Epley manoeuvres.
clinicalPearl: After successful stapedectomy, any sudden sensorineural deterioration warrants urgent investigation for perilymphatic fistula or reparative granuloma — both are treatable if caught early.
keyClue: Successful stapedectomy with closed air-bone gap — counselling must focus on late sensorineural complications that could reverse the surgical gain.
commonExamTrap: Assuming the successful air-bone gap closure means no further monitoring is needed — sensorineural risk persists long-term.
missedConcept: Stapedectomy closes the conductive gap but the cochlea remains vulnerable to perilymphatic leak or inflammatory granuloma around the prosthesis for years post-operatively.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents with an 8-month history of near-constant bilateral nasal congestion. She initially used oxymetazoline nasal spray for 3 days during a cold but has been unable to stop because congestion returns within hours of each dose. She now uses the spray 3–4 times daily. She denies sneezing, watery rhinorrhoea, or itchy eyes. There is no family history of atopy. Nasal endoscopy reveals diffusely erythematous, oedematous turbinates with a beefy-red appearance. Which complication has developed, and what is the most appropriate management?
options: Rhinitis medicamentosa; gradual withdrawal of the decongestant spray with substitution of intranasal corticosteroids
Perennial allergic rhinitis; commence intranasal corticosteroids and antihistamines
Vasomotor rhinitis; treat with ipratropium bromide nasal spray
Nasal polyposis; arrange CT sinuses and refer for FESS
Atrophic rhinitis; commence nasal douching and topical antibiotics
explanation: Rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound rhinitis) is a non-allergic condition caused by prolonged use (>3–5 days) of topical alpha-adrenergic decongestants such as oxymetazoline. The alpha receptors downregulate, causing rebound vasodilation on drug withdrawal. Management involves educating the patient about the rebound phenomenon and tapering or abruptly stopping the decongestant, supported by a short course of intranasal corticosteroids (and occasionally a tapering dose of oral steroids) to manage withdrawal oedema.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: rhinitis medicamentosa from prolonged oxymetazoline use, managed by stopping the decongestant and bridging with intranasal corticosteroids.

Perennial allergic rhinitis features sneezing, watery discharge, and itching and is associated with atopy; this patient's history is clearly linked to decongestant overuse, not allergen sensitisation.
Vasomotor (non-allergic) rhinitis is triggered by environmental stimuli and is not characterised by the rebound pattern tied to a specific nasal spray.
Nasal polyps appear as pale, grey, grape-like masses on endoscopy; the beefy-red, erythematous turbinates described here are characteristic of the vascular engorgement of rhinitis medicamentosa.
Atrophic rhinitis features a dry, crusty, malodorous nasal cavity with mucosal atrophy — the opposite of the oedematous, engorged mucosa seen here.
clinicalPearl: Any patient who cannot stop a topical decongestant after more than 3–5 days likely has rhinitis medicamentosa — transition to intranasal corticosteroids to facilitate withdrawal.
keyClue: 8 months of decongestant use with rebound congestion within hours of each dose and beefy-red turbinates on endoscopy.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing perennial allergic rhinitis and prescribing antihistamines rather than identifying the decongestant as the cause.
missedConcept: Topical decongestants must never be used for more than 3–5 consecutive days; longer use leads to alpha-receptor downregulation and rebound vasodilatation.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old retired teacher presents with a 4-year history of gradual, bilateral hearing difficulty. He reports trouble understanding speech in background noise, asking people to repeat themselves, and difficulty hearing the television at normal volume. His wife says he has become socially withdrawn. He denies vertigo, tinnitus, or ear pain. Otoscopy is normal. Pure-tone audiometry shows a bilateral symmetrical sloping sensorineural hearing loss worst at high frequencies (4–8 kHz), with average thresholds of 45 dB HL across 500–4000 Hz. There is no significant air–bone gap. Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Referral for bilateral hearing aid fitting
MRI internal auditory meati to exclude acoustic neuroma
Cochlear implant assessment as first-line treatment
Reassurance and review in 2 years
Oral corticosteroids for 2 weeks to reverse the hearing loss
explanation: This patient has classic presbycusis (age-related sensorineural hearing loss) — bilateral, symmetrical, high-frequency sloping loss in a 72-year-old with no asymmetry, vertigo, or red flags. With a 45 dB average loss across speech frequencies causing significant communication difficulty and social withdrawal, bilateral hearing aid fitting is the appropriate and evidence-based first step. Untreated presbycusis is associated with cognitive decline and depression, reinforcing the importance of timely rehabilitation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: bilateral hearing aids are first-line management for presbycusis with moderate loss causing functional impairment.

MRI IAM is indicated for asymmetric SNHL, unilateral tinnitus, or rapidly progressive loss to exclude retrocochlear pathology; this patient has bilateral symmetric loss typical of presbycusis and does not meet those criteria.
Cochlear implantation is considered only when hearing aids provide insufficient benefit, typically in severe-to-profound loss; a 45 dB average loss should be trialled with hearing aids first.

Reassurance alone is inappropriate: this patient has moderate functional hearing loss with social withdrawal and psychological impact; 2-year delay will worsen outcomes including cognitive decline risk.
Oral corticosteroids have no role in the management of chronic age-related sensorineural hearing loss; they are used only for acute SSNHL within 72 hours of onset.
clinicalPearl: Presbycusis with moderate bilateral SNHL and functional impairment = bilateral hearing aids; do not delay — untreated hearing loss accelerates cognitive decline.
keyClue: 72-year-old with bilateral symmetrical high-frequency sloping SNHL and social withdrawal — classic presbycusis requiring rehabilitation.
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI for typical symmetric presbycusis — MRI is only indicated for asymmetric or rapidly progressive SNHL.
missedConcept: Timely hearing aid fitting in presbycusis reduces social isolation, depression, and the risk of accelerated cognitive decline.
